section.title: The Division of the Land and the City
section.sectionRationale: The book that opened with the glory of God appearing to a lone exile by a foreign river closes with all twelve tribes settled around a sanctuary at the center of the land, and a city named for nothing else but the fact that the LORD is there. This final chapter is the goal toward which the whole book, and in many ways the whole Bible, has been moving: God dwelling with His people forever.
section.thesis: The whole vision of judgment, exile, and restoration ends not in mere territory but in a name, THE LORD IS THERE, the ancient promise of God's dwelling with His people fulfilled bodily in Christ, Immanuel, God with us, and consummated forever in the new Jerusalem where God Himself dwells with man.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the seven northern tribal portions running from Dan to Judah; the central holy district for sanctuary, priests, Levites, city, and prince; the seven southern tribal portions from Benjamin to Gad; and the city's gates, named for the twelve tribes, closing with its new name, THE LORD IS THERE.

units.label: The northern tribal portions
units.note: Dan to Judah, each bordering the next

verses.text: “Now these are the names of the tribes: At the northern frontier, Dan will have one portion bordering the road of Hethlon to Lebo-hamath and running on to Hazar-enan on the border of Damascus with Hamath to the north, and extending from the east side to the west side.
Asher will have one portion bordering the territory of Dan from east to west.
Naphtali will have one portion bordering the territory of Asher from east to west.
Manasseh will have one portion bordering the territory of Naphtali from east to west.
Ephraim will have one portion bordering the territory of Manasseh from east to west.
Reuben will have one portion bordering the territory of Ephraim from east to west.
Judah will have one portion bordering the territory of Reuben from east to west.

units.label: The central holy district
units.note: sanctuary, priests, Levites, city, and prince

verses.text: Bordering the territory of Judah, from east to west, will be the portion you are to set apart. It will be 25,000 cubits wide, and the length of a tribal portion from east to west. In the center will be the sanctuary.
The special portion you set apart to the LORD shall be 25,000 cubits long and 10,000 cubits wide.

This will be the holy portion for the priests. It will be 25,000 cubits long on the north side, 10,000 cubits wide on the west side, 10,000 cubits wide on the east side, and 25,000 cubits long on the south side. In the center will be the sanctuary of the LORD.
It will be for the consecrated priests, the descendants of Zadok, who kept My charge and did not go astray as the Levites did when the Israelites went astray.
It will be a special portion for them set apart from the land, a most holy portion adjacent to the territory of the Levites.
Bordering the territory of the priests, the Levites shall have an area 25,000 cubits long and 10,000 cubits wide. The whole length will be 25,000 cubits, and the width 10,000 cubits.
They must not sell or exchange any of it, and they must not transfer this best part of the land, for it is holy to the LORD.
The remaining area, 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, will be for common use by the city, for houses, and for pastureland. The city will be in the center of it
and will have these measurements: 4,500 cubits on the north side, 4,500 cubits on the south side, 4,500 cubits on the east side, and 4,500 cubits on the west side.
The pastureland of the city will extend 250 cubits to the north, 250 cubits to the south, 250 cubits to the east, and 250 cubits to the west.

The remainder of the length bordering the holy portion and running adjacent to it will be 10,000 cubits on the east side and 10,000 cubits on the west side. Its produce will supply food for the workers of the city.
The workers of the city who cultivate it will come from all the tribes of Israel.
The entire portion will be a square, 25,000 cubits by 25,000 cubits. You are to set apart the holy portion, along with the city property.

The remaining area on both sides of the holy portion and of the property of the city will belong to the {{nasi|prince}}. He will own the land adjacent to the tribal portions, extending eastward from the 25,000 cubits of the holy district toward the eastern border, and westward from the 25,000 cubits to the western border. And in the center of them will be the holy portion and the sanctuary of the temple.
So the Levitical property and the city property will lie in the center of the area belonging to the prince, the area between the borders of Judah and Benjamin.

units.label: The southern tribal portions
units.note: Benjamin to Gad, each bordering the next

verses.text: As for the rest of the tribes: Benjamin will have one portion extending from the east side to the west side.
Simeon will have one portion bordering the territory of Benjamin from east to west.
Issachar will have one portion bordering the territory of Simeon from east to west.
Zebulun will have one portion bordering the territory of Issachar from east to west.
And Gad will have one portion bordering the territory of Zebulun from east to west.
The southern border of Gad will run from Tamar to the waters of Meribath-kadesh, then along the Brook of Egypt and out to the Great Sea.
This is the land you are to allot as an inheritance to the tribes of Israel, and these will be their portions,” declares the Lord GOD.

units.label: The gates of the city: THE LORD IS THERE
units.note: Yahweh Shammah, the goal of the whole book

verses.text: “These will be the exits of the city: Beginning on the north side, which will be 4,500 cubits long,
the gates of the city will be named after the tribes of Israel. On the north side there will be three gates: the gate of Reuben, the gate of Judah, and the gate of Levi.
On the east side, which will be 4,500 cubits long, there will be three gates: the gate of Joseph, the gate of Benjamin, and the gate of Dan.
On the south side, which will be 4,500 cubits long, there will be three gates: the gate of Simeon, the gate of Issachar, and the gate of Zebulun.
And on the west side, which will be 4,500 cubits long, there will be three gates: the gate of Gad, the gate of Asher, and the gate of Naphtali.
The perimeter of the city will be 18,000 cubits, and from that day on the name of the city will be: {{yahwehShammah|THE LORD IS THERE}}.”

nasi.gloss: Prince, chief, never melek, king. His own territory here still surrounds, rather than replaces, the holy portion at the center, a final picture of authority ordered around God's presence rather than above it.

yahwehShammah.gloss: The LORD is there, Yahweh Shammah. The city's new name, and the closing word of the entire book of Ezekiel, answers every earlier scene of the glory departing with a promise that His presence will never leave again.

basic.context: This final chapter divides the whole land among the twelve tribes, seven portions running north from Dan to Judah, then the central holy district with the sanctuary, the priests' and Levites' land, the city, and the prince's own territory arranged around it, then seven more portions running south from Benjamin to Gad. The city itself gets twelve gates, three on each side, each named for a tribe, so that every tribe has a way in. And then, in the very last line of the book, the city receives its true name, not a dynasty, not a king, but a simple declaration: THE LORD IS THERE. After forty-eight chapters that began with a vision of glory by a foreign river and moved through the departure of that glory from a defiled temple, the whole book ends by answering its own deepest question. Where is God now? Right here, with His people, forever.
basic.hermeneutics: However literally or symbolically one reads the exact tribal boundaries and measurements, which even ancient interpreters recognized do not map cleanly onto the actual geography of Canaan, the chapter's climax is unmistakable and does not depend on resolving those questions: the entire, elaborate vision exists to arrive at one final sentence, a name that declares God's abiding presence with His people.
basic.originalLanguages: The nasi, prince, again never called melek, king, has his own territory arranged around, not replacing, the central sanctuary, consistent with his lay status throughout the vision. The book's final words are Yahweh Shammah, the LORD is there, a name built from the divine name itself joined to the simple adverb 'there,' making God's presence, not any human ruler or dynasty, the city's entire identity.
basic.typology: The city named for God's presence anticipates the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, John 1:14, God's presence no longer symbolized by a name over a city but embodied in a person. The twelve gates, named for the twelve tribes so that every tribe has access, anticipate the twelve gates of the new Jerusalem, named for the twelve tribes of Israel, in Revelation 21:12-13, opened now to people from every nation.
basic.christ: Isaiah had already promised that a virgin would conceive and bear a son, and they will call Him Immanuel, meaning, 'God with us,' Matthew 1:23, the fulfillment in a person of what Ezekiel 48:35 promises in a place. Revelation 21:2-3 shows the new Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from God, and a loud voice from the throne declaring, 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man, and He will dwell with them, and they will be His people, and God Himself will be with them,' the very promise, THE LORD IS THERE, made permanent and unshakable, this time never again to be threatened by departure.
basic.god: God's whole purpose across this long and difficult book has been to dwell with His people, not to abandon them; the glory left in judgment in chapters 8-11 precisely so that it could return in undiminished mercy here, and the entire elaborate ordering of tribes, priesthood, and city exists only to make room for, and to name, His abiding presence.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 25:8 first states God's intention that Israel build Him a sanctuary so that I may dwell among them. Matthew 1:23 identifies Jesus as Immanuel, God with us, fulfilling the promise of this city's name in a person. Revelation 21:3 declares that the dwelling place of God is with man forever. Revelation 21:12-13 shows the new Jerusalem's twelve gates named for the twelve tribes of Israel, echoing Ezekiel 48:31-34.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible can be read as the story of a God who made a good world to dwell with His people, lost that dwelling through sin, and has been working ever since, through tabernacle, temple, exile, and promised return, to dwell with them again. Ezekiel's vision, from the glory departing in judgment to that same glory returning to fill a new temple and finally to name an entire city THE LORD IS THERE, traces the whole arc of that story. It reaches its center in Jesus Christ, Immanuel, God with us in flesh and blood, and its final, unshakable fulfillment in Revelation 21, where God's dwelling place is with man forever, and the need for any temple at all disappears, because the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.

context.literary: The final chapter of Ezekiel and the capstone of the entire temple vision, chapters 40-48, resolving the book's long movement from the glory's departure in judgment to a land, priesthood, prince, and city fully ordered around God's abiding presence.
context.historical: Written for exiles who had lost their land, their temple, and their king, this vision answers with far more than a return to the old arrangement, a completely reordered land and a city whose very name declares that the deepest loss, the departure of God's presence, has been permanently reversed.
context.canonical: Echoes Exodus 25:8's founding purpose for the tabernacle, that I may dwell among them, and directly supplies imagery, the twelve named gates and the promise of God's abiding presence, that Revelation 21 draws on for its own vision of the new Jerusalem.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the entire book of Ezekiel, and its long argument about the departure and return of God's glory, to its final resting point: a land ordered for all twelve tribes, a prince who serves rather than rules over God's presence, and a city whose true identity is simply that the LORD is there.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific prophetic vision of tribal allotment and a named city; it is not a literal surveyor's plan meant to be reconstructed on a map today. Its abiding, non-negotiable truth is theological: the entire purpose of God's redemptive work is to dwell with His people, a truth fulfilled in Christ and consummated in the new creation.

debates.question: Do the tribal boundaries of chapter 48 describe a future, literal reallocation of the land of Israel?

views.label: A future, literal fulfillment
views.case: Some read this as a genuine future geographic and political reordering of Israel's tribal land, part of a wider expectation of restoration still to come.

views.label: A symbolic picture of ordered, complete belonging
views.case: Others note that the neat, symmetrical bands of territory running straight across the land, unlike Israel's actual, uneven historical borders, signal a symbolic ideal, portraying every tribe's equal access to God's presence at the center, a picture whose substance is fulfilled in the new Jerusalem's gates for all twelve tribes, Revelation 21:12-13.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter's function is to picture completeness and equal access to God for the whole covenant people, a hope the New Testament shows fulfilled and widened in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The nasi, prince, is given his own territory arranged around, never replacing, the central sanctuary, keeping him a servant of the order rather than its center. The book's very last words are Yahweh Shammah, the LORD is there, joining the divine name to a simple adverb of place, so that the city's whole identity becomes nothing but the fact of God's presence.

typology.type: The city renamed for God's abiding presence, THE LORD IS THERE
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, John 1:14, and Jesus named Immanuel, God with us, Matthew 1:23.

typology.type: The twelve gates of the city, named for the twelve tribes of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the twelve gates of the new Jerusalem, named for the twelve tribes, Revelation 21:12-13, now open to the nations in Christ.

study.christ: Isaiah had promised that a virgin would conceive and bear a son, and they will call Him Immanuel, meaning, 'God with us,' Matthew 1:23, fulfilling in a person what Ezekiel 48:35 promises in a place. Revelation 21:2-3 shows the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, and a loud voice from the throne declaring, 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man, and He will dwell with them, and they will be His people, and God Himself will be with them and be their God,' the ancient promise, THE LORD IS THERE, made permanent and unshakable at last, never again threatened by departure or exile.
study.god: God's entire purpose across this long and often severe book has been to dwell with His people rather than abandon them forever; the glory departed in judgment in chapters 8-11 precisely so that it could return in undiminished mercy in chapter 43, and the whole final ordering of tribes, priesthood, prince, and city in this chapter exists only to make room for, and finally to name, that abiding presence.

crossReferences.note: God's founding purpose for the tabernacle, that I may dwell among them, the same purpose this final city's name declares fulfilled.
Identifies Jesus as Immanuel, God with us, the personal fulfillment of the city's name, THE LORD IS THERE.
Declares that the dwelling place of God is with man, and He will dwell with them forever, the final, unshakable form of this promise.
Shows the new Jerusalem's twelve gates named for the twelve tribes of Israel, echoing the twelve named gates of Ezekiel 48:31-34.

study.oneStory: The Bible can be read as the story of a God who made a good world to dwell with His people, lost that dwelling through sin, and has worked ever since, through tabernacle, temple, exile, and promised return, to dwell with them again. Ezekiel's vision, moving from the glory's departure in judgment to its return to fill a new temple, and finally to a whole city named THE LORD IS THERE, traces that entire arc. It centers on Jesus Christ, Immanuel, God with us in flesh and blood, and finds its final, unshakable fulfillment in Revelation 21, where the dwelling place of God is with man forever, and no temple is needed at all, because the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.

quiz.q: How many tribal portions run north of the central holy district, from Dan to Judah?

options.text: Seven, Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, and Judah
options.note: 48:1-7 lists exactly these seven tribes running north to south down to the holy district.

options.text: Twelve, including every tribe in a single northern band
options.note: The twelve tribes are split, seven north and five south, of the central district.

options.text: Three, matching the three gates on each side of the city
options.note: The gate count of three per side is separate from the seven northern tribal portions.

options.text: Five, with the rest placed south of the holy district
options.note: Seven tribes are placed north; five, Benjamin through Gad, are placed south, 48:23-27.

quiz.q: Where is the prince's own territory located relative to the holy district and the city?

options.text: On both sides of the holy portion and the city property, bordering the tribal portions
options.note: 48:21 places the prince's land flanking the central district on the east and west.

options.text: Inside the sanctuary grounds themselves, alongside the priests
options.note: The sanctuary and priestly land are explicitly distinct from, and holier than, the prince's surrounding territory.

options.text: Entirely outside the boundaries of the land altogether
options.note: His territory is explicitly within the land, adjacent to the holy district.

options.text: Replacing the Levites' portion after their earlier demotion
options.note: The Levites retain their own distinct portion, 48:13-14; the prince's land is separate from theirs.

quiz.q: How are the city's twelve gates arranged and named?

options.text: Three gates on each of the four sides, each gate named after one of the twelve tribes of Israel
options.note: 48:31-34 lists three gates per side, twelve total, each bearing a tribal name.

options.text: One gate for each cardinal direction, named for the four largest tribes only
options.note: There are twelve gates total, three per side, not merely four gates for four tribes.

options.text: Twelve gates, but left unnamed to avoid tribal favoritism
options.note: Each gate is explicitly named for a specific tribe, 48:31-34.

options.text: Gates named only for Levi, Joseph, and Judah, repeated on every side
options.note: All twelve tribes receive a distinct named gate, not merely these three repeated.

quiz.q: What is the new name given to the city in the final verse of the book, 48:35?

options.text: THE LORD IS THERE
options.note: 48:35 gives this exact name, Yahweh Shammah, as the closing statement of the entire book.

options.text: The City of David, restored
options.note: This traditional name for Jerusalem is not the name given in this verse.

options.text: The city receives no new name; it keeps its former name
options.note: The text explicitly states a new name is given, effective from that day forward.

options.text: The Holy Sanctuary of Zadok
options.note: The name given centers on the LORD's presence, not on the priestly line of Zadok.

quiz.q: How does Revelation 21:3 fulfill the promise named in Ezekiel 48:35?

options.text: A loud voice from the throne declares that the dwelling place of God is with man, and He will dwell with them forever, the same promise made permanent and unshakable
options.note: Revelation 21:3 states this directly, completing what Ezekiel 48:35 names in the city, THE LORD IS THERE.

options.text: It declares that God's presence will again depart, as it did in Ezekiel 8-11
options.note: Revelation 21 describes the opposite, an unending, unshakable dwelling of God with man.

options.text: It limits God's dwelling to a rebuilt physical temple structure alone
options.note: Revelation 21:22 explicitly says no temple is needed, since God and the Lamb are its temple.

options.text: It restricts this promise only to the twelve tribes of Israel by blood
options.note: Revelation 21 broadens this dwelling to the nations, whose glory is brought into the city, Revelation 21:24-26.

questions: The whole elaborate ordering of tribes, priesthood, and prince exists only to arrive at one final name, THE LORD IS THERE. What in your own life is ultimately for the sake of God's presence, rather than an end in itself, once you strip away the details?

Every tribe, without exception, gets its own named gate into the city. Is there a part of your own story or identity you have assumed has no place near God's presence, that this picture of complete, named belonging might correct?

The prince's own territory is arranged around, not replacing, the sanctuary at the center. Where might you need to reorder your own priorities so that God's presence, not your own position or comfort, sits at the center?
Jesus is named Immanuel, God with us, the personal fulfillment of this city's name. How does it change your prayer life this week to remember that God is not distant but literally with you in Christ?
Revelation 21 promises that God's dwelling with His people will never again be threatened by departure. What fear or instability in your own life might this unshakable promise be meant to steady?

translationNotes.note: An embedded line break in the source text between 'names of the tribes:' and 'At the northern frontier' has been flattened into continuous prose here, matching the sentence's original sense.
The em dash before 'the area between the borders of Judah and Benjamin' has been rendered as a comma here without changing the meaning.
An embedded line break in the source text between 'the rest of the tribes:' and 'Benjamin will have' has been flattened into continuous prose here.
An embedded line break in the source text between 'the exits of the city:' and 'Beginning on the north side' has been flattened into continuous prose here.

'THE LORD IS THERE' translates the Hebrew Yahweh Shammah, a compound name joining the divine name to the adverb 'there'; a trailing space before the closing quotation mark in the source text has been trimmed here without altering the wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 48:1-35. The em dash at 48:22 replaced with a comma; embedded line breaks within 48:1, 48:23, and 48:30 flattened into continuous prose; a stray trailing space before the closing quotation mark in 48:35 trimmed. No em or en dashes used elsewhere. Web search this session (Truth For Life, Desiring God, Gospel Coalition, Gospel in Life) found no sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) that specifically expounds Ezekiel 48; the Truth For Life page for this chapter lists only broad, multi-book overview messages, not a targeted exposition of this passage, so sermon status is none.
